title: Melee behavior
principles: Melee analysis must separate normal attacks, heavy attacks, slide attacks, slams, and heavy slams when their supplied values differ.
Attack speed affects repeated normal-attack rhythm but must not be treated as the complete animation speed of every stance action.
Range describes nominal melee reach and should not be converted into reliable group coverage without stance, arc, or movement evidence.
Follow-through describes retained damage across successive enemies in a swing path and is not the same as Punch Through or radial damage.
Heavy-attack suitability depends on heavy damage, wind-up, critical and status relationships, and the cost or activation system when supplied.
Slam radius supplies spatial reach, while slam radial damage supplies the corresponding damage component; neither alone establishes area control.
Combo duration affects how long accumulated combo can be retained but does not prove fast combo generation.
Blocking angle is a defensive handling field and should not be used as offensive damage evidence.

normal_attack_focused: The supplied profile primarily supports repeated normal melee attacks.
heavy_attack_capable: The supplied heavy-attack values provide explicit evidence for heavy-attack analysis.
Wind-up and supported relationships determine practical suitability.
slam_capable: The supplied slam fields provide explicit radial or impact behavior.
Normal slam and heavy slam must remain separate.
mixed_melee: The weapon has meaningful evidence across several melee attack families.
The primary job should identify which family is best supported.
undetermined: The supplied melee fields are insufficient to identify a dominant melee pattern.

exceptions: Do not infer stance multipliers or forced status effects.
Do not infer attack arcs from range alone.
Do not call every melee weapon heavy-attack focused because heavy-attack fields exist.
Do not merge slam radial damage with normal attack damage.
Do not infer combo efficiency without combo-generation evidence.
